nativeRange.habitat: Juveniles found in estuaries, mangrove creeks, tidal rivers, coastal freshwater lower reaches, and sewage-influenced canals; adults migrate to coastal reef flats, harbours, and open marine coastal waters

decorPreferences: robust rock or coral rubble hardscape (salt-tolerant)
minimal or no live plants (nibbled and incompatible with brackish/marine transition)
brackish-tolerant artificial plants as cover if desired
strong filtration with good surface agitation — Scats are heavy bioload producers

diet.notes: Highly opportunistic omnivore; wild fish are notorious for consuming sewage and organic detritus as well as algae, plant matter, invertebrates, and small fish. In captivity accepts high-quality marine pellets and flake, frozen foods (brine shrimp, mysis, bloodworms, krill), blanched vegetables, spirulina, and seaweed. Herbivorous component is important — supplement regularly with algae wafers, nori, and vegetable matter. Feed 2–3 times daily; Scats are voracious and will overeat. Live or frozen bloodworms should be offered sparingly as a treat.

goodWith: robust euryhaline species sharing the salinity progression
avoidWith: small freshwater fish (incompatible long-term salinity needs; predation of very small fish)
purely freshwater species once brackish transition begins

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo gallery for Scatophagus argus (species ID 1118). Multiple images of spotted juvenile and adult specimens. Reviewer should select best lateral view showing argus-pattern spotting.
Wikimedia Commons category for Scatophagus argus; freely licensed images of aquarium and wild specimens. Spot pattern varies with age — juveniles show larger, bolder spots.

summary: The Scat (Scatophagus argus) is a boldly spotted Indo-Pacific fish sold as a juvenile in freshwater but requiring a long-term transition to brackish and ultimately marine conditions. Active, omnivorous, and fast-growing to ~30 cm, it carries mildly venomous dorsal spines and demands specialist euryhaline husbandry — not suitable for a standard freshwater community tank.
careNotes: The Scat is one of the archetypal euryhaline trap species sold in the freshwater hobby: juveniles enter retail stores in freshwater sections, yet in the wild they migrate progressively from estuarine nursery grounds to full marine coastal waters as they mature. Committed long-term keeping requires a deliberate salinity progression — starting at low brackish (SG 1.003–1.005) for juveniles, increasing incrementally over months and years toward SG 1.010–1.015 for sub-adults, and ultimately full marine (SG 1.020–1.025) for adults. Specimens kept indefinitely in pure freshwater typically show chronic stress, reduced disease resistance, and shortened lifespan. A minimum group of three is strongly recommended; solitary Scats are prone to stress and may become more aggressive. Given an adult size of 25–30 cm, a long-footprint tank of at least 380 L is the practical minimum for a small group. Filtration must be robust: Scats are heavy waste producers with a natural affinity for nutrient-rich environments, and water quality must be maintained to prevent disease. Handle with care — the dorsal and anal fin spines are mildly venomous and can cause a painful puncture wound; use a net with thick mesh or a container when handling. Do not keep with live aquatic plants: Scats will eat or uproot virtually all plant species, and brackish/marine salinity levels are incompatible with most aquatic plants regardless. Aquarium décor should be salt-tolerant hardscape. Temperature should remain stable at 22–28°C across all life stages. Colour and spot intensity vary with life stage and salinity; juveniles typically display bolder black spots on a reddish or golden background that fades to silver-grey with brownish spotting in adults. The species name argus refers to the many-eyed giant of Greek mythology, a nod to the spotted patterning. Closely related S. tetracanthus (African Scat) has similar husbandry requirements. See also: [mono] (fw-fish-411) — shares the same retail freshwater-juvenile / brackish-marine-adult trap; [colombian-shark-catfish] (fw-fish-304) — another euryhaline species with the same life-history pattern.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Scatophagus argus has not been reliably documented in the aquarium hobby. In the wild, spawning is presumed to occur in coastal or offshore marine waters. Eggs and larvae are planktonic. External sexing is not reliably possible, though gravid females may appear noticeably fuller-bodied. The species has been bred commercially in pond aquaculture operations in Southeast Asia using hormone induction, but these methods are impractical for home aquarists. No amateur captive breeding protocol exists; trade specimens are wild-caught juveniles or pond-raised fish harvested from estuarine nursery areas.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Scatophagus argus
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed adult size (to 30 cm TL), temperature 22–28°C, pH 7.0–8.5, native Indo-Pacific range, estuarine-to-marine life history, venomous dorsal spines, shoaling requirement, semi-aggressive temperament, and brackish/marine transition husbandry detail. Diet confirmed as opportunistic omnivore with strong herbivorous component.

additional.name: FishBase — Scatophagus argus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 38 cm TL reported in wild; 30 cm used as practical aquarium maximum), distribution map confirming Indo-Pacific range from India to Australia and Japan, taxonomy (Scatophagidae, Perciformes), depth range (0–15 m), habitat (estuaries, coastal mangroves, reef-associated adults), euryhaline classification, and venomous spine notation. Temperature range 22–28°C confirmed.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Scat Fish Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-oriented overview. Confirmed brackish-to-marine transition requirement, minimum tank size (100+ gal for adults), omnivore diet with heavy plant/algae component, compatibility pairing with Monos and archerfish, venomous spine handling warning, and the retail freshwater-labeling trap. Highlighted long-term salinity progression commitment.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Scatophagus argus species profile
additional.notes: UK trade source. Corroborated estuarine juvenile / coastal marine adult life history, salinity progression, large adult size (25–30 cm in captivity), the venomous dorsal and anal spine caution, and the recommendation to keep with Monos and other euryhaline species. Noted that Scats will demolish aquatic plants entirely.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Brackish Fishes: The Scat
additional.notes: In-depth hobbyist article detailing salinity progression protocol, water chemistry across life stages, compatibility with Monos and archerfish, venomous spine handling guidance, diet (omnivore with strong vegetable emphasis), and long-term brackish/marine husbandry. Confirmed no reliable amateur captive breeding records. Noted 8–15 year lifespan with proper marine transition.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable amateur captive breeding protocol documented. Wild spawning presumed in coastal marine waters; planktonic eggs and larvae. Sexes are not reliably distinguishable externally. Commercial aquaculture uses hormone induction in ponds — impractical for home aquarists. Trade specimens are wild-caught or pond-raised estuarine juveniles.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Sold as freshwater but is BRACKISH→MARINE at adult (similar to Mono fw-fish-411 and Colombian Shark Catfish fw-fish-304). Long-term requires transition to brackish (SG 1.015+) then marine. ~30cm. VENOMOUS DORSAL SPINES.
